summary:
Glass-ionomer cement has a more favorable modulus of elasticity that better matches dental tissue compared to other options, enhancing integration and reducing risk of complications when used alongside complex amalgam restorations due to improved mechanical compatibility. Polycarboxylate cement offers benefits but falls short regarding this specific critical property necessary for effective support structures within such intricate restorations scenarios. The primary consideration should be achieving close mimicry of natural tooth properties via material choice; hence glass-ionomers stand out as superior based upon their elastic behavior most similar to human teeth structure at these interfaces requiring high precision and durability. To directly answer your query succinctly while adhering strictly per instructions provided:

Glass-ionomer cement exhibits an ideal modulus of elasticity closest resembling native dentine among available choices, thereby providing excellent supportive qualities essential especially against stresses encountered from adjacent large-scale amalgams needing robust anchorage without compromising long-term structural integrity or biocompatibility requirements dictated by modern dental practice
